Article.ArticleTitle: Unraveling the flame retardant mechanism of protein-rich matrix recovered from anaerobic sludge: The governing role of microstructural characteristics.

Abstract.AbstractText: Upcycling waste sludge into valuable products offers promising benefits for achieving carbon neutrality. Although sewage sludge has been extensively studied, the potential of floc sludge recovered from anaerobic fermentation systems remains unclear. In this study, the effects of sludge type and recovery strategy on the flame-retardant potential of protein-rich matrix (PRM) recovered from anaerobic sludge were investigated. The PRM recovered from methanogenic sludge (AnMS) exhibited a shorter smoldering time than that recovered from acidogenic sludge (AnAS). This disparity was attributed to distinct microstructural characteristics. AnMS-PRM was enriched in polymeric aromatic proteins, which facilitated the formation of more high-thermal-resistance N-heterocyclic compounds (e.g., pyridine-N and quaternary-N) during combustion. Meanwhile, the extracellular PRM (E-PRM) recovered via mild thermo-alkaline treatment was rich in tryptophan and hyaluronic acid-like compounds (such as β-D-mannuronic acid and α-L-guluronic acid) secreted by microbes under a long sludge retention time of 25 days, consequently promoting the formation of a protein-polysaccharide crosslinked structural network with gel-like properties. This resulted in the increase of limiting oxygen index (LOI) of cotton fabric from 18.6 % (pristine one) to 24.4 % (AnMS-E-PRM modified one) with expanded char layer formation. In contrast, AnAS-PRM exhibited weak thermochemical stability owing to its loose microstructure dominated by macromolecular and aliphatic proteins. Notably, the abundant basic amino acids in the E-PRM of AnAS were decomposed into low-thermal-resistance pyrrole-N compounds during combustion, resulting in the lowest LOI of 21.6 % and the formation of separated char fragments. These findings elucidate the influence of sludge type and recovery strategy on the flame-retardant potential of PRM and offer novel perspectives for anaerobic sludge valorization.
